did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically comparing five architectures (ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2) to classify six facial skin conditions (rosacea, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, lupus erythematosus, basal cell melanoma, and squamous cell melanoma) using a dataset of 2,656 facial images from Xiangya-Derm.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a dataset from Xiangya-Derm, the comparison of five specific CNN architectures (ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2), and the reporting of new performance metrics (recall and precision) on a test dataset of 388 facial images. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results and algorithmic validation.

title: A DEEP LEARNING ANALYSIS ON FACIAL SKIN USING CNN
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Systems, Smart and Green Technologies (ICISSGT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying six common facial skin conditions—rosacea, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, lupus erythematosus, basal cell melanoma, and squamous cell melanoma—using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The researchers constructed a specialized dataset of 2,656 facial images from the Xiangya-Derm clinical imaging repository, which contains over 150,000 images of 543 skin conditions. Five CNN architectures (ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2) were compared using transfer learning on an independent test dataset of 388 facial images. The Inception-ResNet-v2 model achieved the highest average precision of 77.0% and demonstrated strong recall rates for specific conditions, including 92.9% for lupus erythematosus and 89.2% for basal cell melanoma. The findings indicate that while CNNs are effective for facial skin analysis, specific model adaptations are necessary for different body regions to improve diagnostic accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six facial skin conditions studied; 689 images total (578 train, 100 test) in the Xiangya-Derm dataset subset.

all_task_types.target: Six discrete facial skin conditions (rosacea, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, lupus erythematosus, basal cell melanoma, and squamous cell melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin Disease Classification' and uses CNNs to 'identify six current facial skin conditions'. The models output 'six confidence labels' via a softmax classifier. Performance is measured using Recall and Precision for each of the six classes.

datasets_used.name: Xiangya-Derm
datasets_used.description: A clinical imaging dataset collected by the Department of Dermatology at Central South University’s Xiangya School of Medicine. It is described as the largest clinical image dataset for skin conditions intended for computer-aided diagnosis (CADx), comprising 150,223 clinical images encompassing 543 distinct skin conditions. The study specifically extracted a subset of facial images representing six common skin conditions.

use.training: Used to train five CNN architectures (ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2) for classifying facial skin conditions.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the trained models, specifically an independent test dataset of 388 facial images.
use.other: The full Xiangya-Derm dataset (150,223 images) was referenced as the source, but the study focused on a specific subset of 2,656 facial images for the primary analysis. The paper also mentions examining public datasets (DermlS, AtlasDerm, ISIC Archive, Derm101, Dermnet) to highlight the lack of facial-specific data, but these were not used for model training or testing in this study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares model performance using recall and precision metrics without statistical significance tests or p-values.

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset - Test Set).description: Evaluation of five CNN architectures on an independent test dataset of 388 facial images representing six skin conditions (BCC, LE, ROS, SK, AK, SCC). Two experimental settings were reported: direct training on facial images and transfer learning (pretraining on other body areas then fine-tuning).

Inception-ResNet-v2 (Transfer Learning).setting: Transfer Learning (Pretrained on other body areas)

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset - Test Set).notes: The Inception-ResNet-v2 model achieved the highest average precision (77.0%) and average recall (77.0%) among models trained directly on facial images. Transfer learning generally outperformed direct facial training. Most misclassifications of Actinic Keratosis (AK) were classified as Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) due to clinical parallels and sample size differences. The best model attained recalls of 92.9% for lupus erythematosus, 89.2% for basal cell melanoma, and 84.3% for seborrheic keratosis in the transfer learning setting.

architecture_used.main_model: Five CNN architectures: ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet; Transfer learning applied by pretraining on data from other body areas before fine-tuning on facial images

architecture_details.ResNet50: Improves connectivity between shallow and deep layers by transmitting information directly from shallow to deep subcaste
architecture_details.Inception V3: Uses multiple kernels with different shapes; inception block serves as introductory unit
architecture_details.DenseNet-121: Establishes connections between every two layers; thick connections for feature reuse
architecture_details.Xception: Depthwise separable convolution module; improves upon Inception by divorcing cross-channel and spatial correlations
architecture_details.Inception-ResNet-v2: Hybrid architecture combining Inception and ResNet features; includes residual condition

training_details.loss_function: Cost function with different weights assigned to different ailments to address data imbalance

classification_or_output_head.details: 1024-dimensional completely connected subcaste to induce six confidence labels for six distinct facial skin conditions
classification_or_output_head.modifications: First completely connected subcaste preceding the final convolutional subcaste replaced with global average pooling and a 1x1 convolution to reduce parameters and retain spatial information

preprocessing_techniques: Replacement of the first fully connected layer preceding the final convolutional layer with global average pooling and a 1x1 convolution to reduce parameters and retain spatial information
Use of a logistic regression classifier or softmax with a 1024-dimensional fully connected layer for output

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Standard preprocessing styles applied to the images during training.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class balancing via cost function weighting
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Different weights were assigned to different ailments in the cost function to address data imbalance.

conclusions: The study successfully demonstrated that Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are capable of identifying six common facial skin conditions (rosacea, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, lupus erythematosus, basal cell melanoma, and squamous cell melanoma) using clinical images.

Among the five tested architectures (ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet-121, Xception, and Inception-ResNet-v2), Inception-ResNet-v2 achieved the highest performance with an average recall of 77.0% and average precision of 70.8% when trained directly on facial images.
Transfer learning models, which were pretrained on data from other body areas before fine-tuning on facial images, generally outperformed models trained solely on facial images.
The best-performing transfer learning model achieved high recall rates for specific conditions: 92.9% for lupus erythematosus, 89.2% for basal cell melanoma, and 84.3% for seborrheic keratosis.

A significant limitation identified was the misclassification of Actinic Keratosis (AK) as Seborrheic Keratosis (SK), which was attributed to clinical parallels between the conditions (e.g., flat pustules, brown rashes) and a disparity in sample sizes (more SK images than AK images) in the dataset.

The study highlighted a gap in existing public datasets regarding facial-specific skin condition data, necessitating the creation of a specialized dataset from the Xiangya-Derm repository to address the complexity of facial clinical imaging.
important_remarks: The study is limited to six specific facial skin conditions (rosacea, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, lupus erythematosus, basal cell melanoma, and squamous cell melanoma), restricting generalizability to other dermatological pathologies.

The dataset is derived exclusively from the Xiangya-Derm repository (Central South University, China), introducing potential geographic and demographic bias that may limit external validity for non-Asian populations.

There is a significant class imbalance in the dataset, particularly for Actinic Keratosis (277 images) compared to Seborrheic Keratosis (1,127 images), which contributed to the misclassification of AK as SK.

The paper acknowledges that distinguishing between clinically similar conditions like Actinic Keratosis and Seborrheic Keratosis is challenging at small data scales, leading to notable confusion between these classes.

No external validation was performed; the model was tested only on an independent subset of the same source dataset (Xiangya-Derm), raising concerns about overfitting to specific imaging protocols or patient demographics.
The study lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the reported performance metrics, relying solely on point estimates of recall and precision.

The paper does not report standard accuracy, sensitivity, or specificity metrics, making it difficult to compare results directly with other dermatological AI studies that typically use these standard benchmarks.

Transfer learning from other body areas outperformed direct facial training, suggesting that facial skin analysis requires specific adaptations and that models trained on non-facial data may not be directly applicable without fine-tuning.

The study highlights a gap in public datasets regarding facial-specific skin conditions, noting that existing datasets like Dermnet have very few facial images, which limits the ability to train robust general-purpose models.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks beyond stating that training and test sets were insulated based on distinct cases; it does not detail how patient identity was verified to ensure no overlap.

The clinical relevance is limited by the retrospective nature of the dataset and the lack of information on disease severity, stage, or patient history, which are critical for clinical decision-making.
The study does not discuss deployment concerns, such as computational requirements, inference speed, or integration into clinical workflows, which are essential for practical application.
